Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [http://www.cnet-sc.ne.jp/nisigori/nisigori.htm Nishikiori Shrine Information Page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto-related navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nishikiori Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tondabayashi]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Buildings)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:錦織神社]]

Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [http://www.cnet-sc.ne.jp/nisigori/nisigori.htm Nishikiori Shrine Information Page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto-related navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nishikiori Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tondabayashi]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Buildings)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Nishikori Shrine-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:錦織神社]]

{{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [http://www.cnet-sc.ne.jp/nisigori/nisigori.htm Nishikiori Shrine Information Page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto-related navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nishikiori Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tondabayashi]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (Buildings)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nishikori Shrine-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:錦織神社]]

Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': {{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * {{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [http://www.cnet-sc.ne.jp/nisigori/nisigori.htm Nishikiori Shrine Information Page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto-related navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nishikiori Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tondabayashi]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': {{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [http://www.cnet-sc.ne.jp/nisigori/nisigori.htm Nishikiori Shrine Information Page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto-related navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nishikiori Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka revision.text:
{{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * [http://www.cnet-sc.ne.jp/nisigori/nisigori.htm Nishikiori Shrine Information Page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto-related navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nishikiori Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tondabayashi]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:錦織神社]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nishikori Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': {{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * {{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * {{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] * Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tondabayashi]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] {{Otheruses|A Shinto shrine in Tondabayashi, Osaka|Other uses}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nishikiori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja01.JPG|250px]]
Main Shrine (center) and auxiliary shrines (sides) | Address = Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_minutes = 29 | Latitude_seconds = 43.2 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude_minutes = 35 | Longitude_seconds = 19.2 | E = E | Country = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}} | Shrine_rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | consecration_year = Unknown (estimated mid-[[Heian period]]) | Main_building = Nishikiori-zukuri | Annual_festival = Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) }} '''Nishikiori Shrine''' (錦織神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Tondabayashi|ja|富田林市}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]], Japan. Formerly classified as a [[gōsha]], the shrine is locally referred to as "Nishikori," "Nishikoori," or "Nishigori." Along with {{ill|Takesuimori Shrine|ja|建水分神社|1=Take Mikumari Shrine}} in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka|ja|千早赤阪村}} and {{ill|Migukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} in Tondabayashi, it is one of the "Three Mikumari Shrines of Kawachi Province." == Deities == * Primary deities: {{ill|Susanoo|ja|建速素戔嗚命}}, {{ill|Hachiman|ja|品陀別命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}} == History == The region surrounding the shrine was once called "Nishikori-no-kori" and is believed to have been settled by people from [[Baekje]] who brought advanced textile techniques. Originally known as "Nigori Shrine," it served as the clan shrine of the {{ill|Nishikori clan|ja|錦部氏}}. While the exact date of establishment is unknown, archaeological findings from the 1935 restoration suggest it dates back to the mid-[[Heian period]] or earlier, based on the discovery of roof tiles from that era.[http://travel-minakawa.com/2018/05/09/nishikiorijinja/ Nishikiori Shrine – A Shrine Influencing Nikko Toshogu Design – Tondabayashi City Nishikiori Shrine Access and Goshuin], Retrieved February 14, 2019 The shrine was also called "Nigori Tennō" and "Mizugori Tennō." During the Heian period, the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|ja|真言宗}} temple {{ill|Konrinji|ja|金輪寺}} was established as its {{ill|jinguji|ja|神宮寺}}. Due to its location near the {{ill|Ishikawa River (Osaka)|ja|石川 (大阪府)}}, a tributary of the {{ill|Yamato River|ja|大和川}}, Nishikiori Shrine has long been associated with the "Three Mikomari Shrines of Kawachi Province." Its main hall, rebuilt in 1363 by Mitsuyoshi Sadayuki, is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji era}}, following the government-mandated {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} and {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the jinguji temple Konrinji was abolished. In 1872, the shrine was designated a [[gōsha]], and it adopted its current name in 1907 when it became a shrine for government offerings. == Grounds == [[File:Nishikiori-Jinja02.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Torii at the start of the approach
(To the left of this photo is the gate of {{ill|Tondabayashi Municipal Kawanishi Elementary School|ja|富田林市立川西小学校}})]] The shrine is accessed through a large stone torii near the gate of Kawanishi Elementary School. A 150-meter approach leads to the main grounds.[https://www.city.tondabayashi.lg.jp/site/bunkazai/2523.html Nishikiori Shrine – Tondabayashi City Cultural Properties Division], Retrieved February 14, 2019 * '''Main Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1363. Nishikiori-zukuri style with an intricate roof structure featuring {{ill|chidori-hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and {{ill|karahafu|ja|唐破風}}. The design inspired later shrine architecture, including {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}. * '''Torii''' * '''Prayer Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Memorial Monument''' - Dedicated to the {{ill|Tenchū-gumi Incident|ja|天誅組の変}} and the shrine’s historical ties to the movement. == Sub-shrines == === Auxiliary Shrines === * '''Tenjin Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, and {{ill|Ebisu|ja|恵比寿神}}. Built in 1480. * '''Kasuga Shrine''' (Important Cultural Property) - Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大明神}} and {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉神}}. Built in 1480. === Associated Shrines === * '''Kotohiragu''' * '''Benzaiten Shrine''' == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === Designated Important Cultural Properties include: * Main Shrine * Tenjin Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) * Kasuga Shrine (Auxiliary Shrine) == festivals == * Autumn Grand Festival (2nd Saturday of October) == Visitor Information == '''Address''': Miyakōda-chō 9-46, Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture '''Access''': Approximately 3 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kawanishi Station (Kintetsu Nagano Line)|ja|川西駅 (大阪府)}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Nagano Line|ja|近鉄長野線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Nishikiori-Jinja}} * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/tondabayasi-city/m15_02_tonndabayasi_nisigoori.html Nishikiori Shrine – Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association] *
